alabama.size: not applicable; small employers (fewer than 20 employees) are not covered by any Alabama state continuation law
alabama.who: No state continuation law. Employees of employers with 20 or more employees rely on federal COBRA; employees of sub-20 employers generally have no state-mandated continuation right in Alabama.
alabama.statute: none identified in the Alabama Insurance Code (Ala. Code Title 27)
alabama.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Alabama does not run its own state-based exchange).

alaska.size: not applicable; small employers (fewer than 20 employees) are not covered by any Alaska state continuation law
alaska.who: No state continuation law. Employees of employers with 20 or more employees rely on federal COBRA; sub-20 employees generally have no state-mandated continuation right in Alaska.
alaska.statute: none identified in the Alaska Insurance Code (Alaska Stat. Title 21)
alaska.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Alaska does not run its own state-based exchange).

arizona.law: Arizona Continuation of Small Group Coverage
arizona.size: small employers averaging at least 1 but fewer than 20 eligible employees in the preceding calendar year (below the federal COBRA threshold)
arizona.who: An enrollee (employee covered at least 3 months before a qualifying event) and qualified dependents (spouse/dependent child). Qualifying events: termination (other than gross misconduct) or reduction in hours, divorce/separation, death of the enrollee, Medicare eligibility, or a child losing dependent status. Base period is 18 months, with an 11-month disability extension (up to 29 months) and a.
arizona.statute: Ariz. Rev. Stat. section 20-2330 (Continuation of small group coverage)
arizona.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Arizona does not run its own state-based exchange).

arkansas.size: applies to group accident and health policies providing hospital, surgical, or major medical coverage (not limited to sub-20 employers); functions as state continuation, generally used where federal COBRA does not apply
arkansas.who: A certificate holder, member, or spouse (and eligible dependents) continuously insured under the group policy for the 3-month period before the qualifying event whose coverage would otherwise terminate due to termination of employment/membership or a change in marital status may continue coverage. Continuation ends at the earliest of 120 days after it begins, Medicare eligibility, or policy.
arkansas.statute: Ark. Code section 23-86-114 (Continuation of coverage beyond termination of employment, change in marital status)
arkansas.market: State-based marketplace on the federal platform: Arkansas Health Insurance Marketplace (My Arkansas Insurance); consumers historically enroll via HealthCare.gov.

california.size: employers with 2 to 19 eligible employees (below the 20-employee federal COBRA threshold); also extends federal COBRA beneficiaries to a combined 36 months
california.who: Employees and their dependents (qualified beneficiaries) of a group covered by a health care service plan or insurer whose employer had 2 to 19 eligible employees and is not subject to federal COBRA. Qualifying events include termination or reduction in hours, death of the employee, divorce or legal separation, Medicare entitlement, and loss of dependent status.
california.statute: Cal. Health & Safety Code section 1366.20 et seq. (health care service plans, DMHC); Cal. Ins. Code section 10128.50 et seq. (disability/health insurers, CDI)
california.market: State-based marketplace: Covered California (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

colorado.size: group health benefit plans issued to employers with 20 or fewer employees (small employers not subject to federal COBRA)
colorado.who: An employee or dependent continuously covered under the group plan for at least 6 months immediately before termination may continue coverage for up to 18 months after loss of coverage (or until eligible for other group coverage, whichever is first), following termination of employment, death of the employee, or a change in marital/civil-union status. Required premium must be paid through the.
colorado.market: State-based marketplace: Connect for Health Colorado (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

connecticut.size: fully-insured employer groups with fewer than 20 employees are covered by CT state continuation (exempt from federal COBRA); the 30-month maximum period applies to Connecticut fully-insured plans of any size
connecticut.who: Employees/certificate holders (and dependents) who lose coverage due to layoff, reduction of hours, leave of absence, or termination of employment other than for gross misconduct may elect continuation. Effective May 5, 2010 (Public Act 10-13) the maximum continuation period was expanded to 30 months for Connecticut fully-insured plans.
connecticut.statute: Conn. Gen. Stat. section 38a-538 (group plan continuation) and section 38a-554 (continuation requirements); Public Act 10-13 (30-month expansion)
connecticut.market: State-based marketplace: Access Health CT (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

delaware.law: Delaware Mini-COBRA (Small Employer Group Health Policies)
delaware.size: small employers that normally employed between 1 and 19 employees on a typical business day during the preceding year (below the federal COBRA threshold)
delaware.who: A covered employee or eligible dependent continuously insured under the group policy (or a replaced policy) during the entire 3-month period ending with termination. Qualifying events: death of the covered employee, termination (other than gross misconduct) or reduction of hours, divorce or legal separation, Medicare entitlement, a child losing dependent status, or employer bankruptcy..
delaware.statute: 18 Del. C. section 3571F (Mini-COBRA small employer group health policies)
delaware.market: Uses HealthCare.gov as a state-based marketplace on the federal platform (Delaware Health Insurance Marketplace / Choose Health Delaware); enrollment via HealthCare.gov.

florida.law: Florida Health Insurance Coverage Continuation Act (mini-COBRA)
florida.size: small employers with fewer than 20 employees (2 to 19), not subject to federal COBRA
florida.who: Eligible employees and dependents (qualified beneficiaries) under a small employer group health plan following qualifying events: termination of employment (other than gross misconduct), reduction in hours, divorce, or death of the covered employee. Up to 18 months, extendable to 29 months on an SSA disability determination.
florida.statute: Fla. Stat. section 627.6692 (Florida Health Insurance Coverage Continuation Act)
florida.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Florida does not run its own state-based exchange).

georgia.size: applies to group accident and sickness contracts generally (not limited to sub-20 employers); functions as state continuation for those not eligible for federal COBRA
georgia.who: A group member (and dependents) continuously covered for at least 6 months before termination whose coverage would otherwise end may continue for the remainder of the month in which termination occurs plus 3 additional months. Not available if employment was terminated for cause, if the member failed to pay a required contribution, or if the group contract/plan terminated entirely or for the.
georgia.statute: O.C.G.A. section 33-24-21.1 (Group accident and sickness contracts; conversion privilege and continuation right provisions)
georgia.market: State-based marketplace: Georgia Access (Georgia moved off HealthCare.gov to its own state exchange platform).

hawaii.law: Hawaii Prepaid Health Care Act - Continuation in case of inability to earn wages (disability continuation, not a true termination-based mini-COBRA)
hawaii.size: all employers subject to the Prepaid Health Care Act (generally any employer with employees working 20+ hours/week); this is not a sub-20 small-employer mini-COBRA
hawaii.who: An employee who is hospitalized or otherwise prevented by sickness from working. The employer must keep contributing its pre-sickness share of the premium so the employee can continue coverage, for up to 3 months following the month the employee became hospitalized/disabled, or the period the employer pays regular wages, whichever is longer.
hawaii.statute: Haw. Rev. Stat. section 393-15 (Continuation of coverage in case of inability to earn wages), part of the Prepaid Health Care Act (HRS ch. 393)
hawaii.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (Hawaii shut down its state exchange, the Hawaii Health Connector, and uses the federal platform).

idaho.law: none (no general termination-based state continuation / mini-COBRA statute)
idaho.size: not applicable; Idaho does not impose a general small-employer continuation mandate
idaho.who: Idaho has no general mini-COBRA giving terminated employees of sub-20 employers a COBRA-style continuation right. Idaho Code 41-2213 only requires a reasonable extension of benefits for individuals who are TOTALLY DISABLED at the time the group policy is discontinued (at least 12 months of benefits for the disabling condition) - it is not a termination-triggered continuation right.
idaho.statute: no general continuation statute; Idaho Code section 41-2213 provides only an extension of benefits for totally disabled individuals (Title 41, Chapter 22); Idaho Code Title 41, Chapter 47 (Small Employer Health Insurance Availability Act) governs guaranteed availability and rating, not continuation
idaho.market: State-based marketplace: Your Health Idaho (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

illinois.law: Illinois Continuation of Coverage (Illinois Insurance Code)
illinois.size: applies to fully-insured group accident and health policies and HMO contracts regardless of employer size (not limited to sub-20 groups); functions as state continuation for groups not covered by, or in addition to, federal COBRA
illinois.who: An employee or member (and dependents) continuously covered under the group policy for at least 3 months whose coverage ends due to termination of employment or reduction in hours may continue coverage for up to 12 months. Separate spousal continuation provisions (215 ILCS 5/367.2) allow a spouse and dependents to continue coverage after divorce or the employee's death.
illinois.statute: 215 ILCS 5/367e (insurance companies); 215 ILCS 125/4-9.2 (HMOs); spousal continuation at 215 ILCS 5/367.2
illinois.market: Illinois is transitioning to its own state-based marketplace (Get Covered Illinois); it has operated as a state-based marketplace on the federal platform. Consumers currently enroll via.

indiana.law: Indiana Small Employer Group Health Insurance - Continuing Coverage
indiana.size: small employers under the Indiana Small Employer Group Health Insurance law (generally small employers not subject to federal COBRA)
indiana.who: An eligible employee employed by the same small employer for at least 1 year and continuously covered under the plan for at least 90 days (or such an employee's dependent) who loses coverage. Qualifying events track federal COBRA: termination of employment, reduction of hours, dissolution of marriage, or a dependent attaining the plan's limiting age. Continuation lasts up to 12 months.
indiana.statute: Ind. Code section 27-8-15-31.1 (Continuing coverage), within the Small Employer Group Health Insurance chapter (IC 27-8-15)
indiana.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Indiana does not run its own state-based exchange).

iowa.law: Iowa Continuation of Group Health Insurance
iowa.size: applies to group health insurance policies generally (Chapter 509B is not limited to sub-20 employers); functions as state continuation, commonly used where federal COBRA does not apply
iowa.who: An employee or member continuously insured under the group policy (and any policy it replaced) during the entire 3-month period immediately preceding termination whose coverage would otherwise terminate due to termination of employment or membership. Continuation ends 9 months after coverage would otherwise have terminated.
iowa.statute: Iowa Code ch. 509B, esp. section 509B.3 (Continuation of benefits)
iowa.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Iowa does not run its own state-based exchange).

kansas.size: group sickness and accident (hospital/surgical/major medical) policies; used primarily by small employers with fewer than 20 employees not subject to federal COBRA
kansas.who: An employee, member, or covered dependent whose insurance is terminated for any reason (including discontinuance of the group policy) who was continuously insured for at least 3 months immediately before termination may continue coverage under the group policy for 18 months, with a conversion policy option at the end of the period.
kansas.statute: Kan. Stat. Ann. section 40-2209 (group sickness and accident insurance; continuation and converted policies)
kansas.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Kansas does not run its own state-based exchange).

kentucky.size: fully-insured group plans; primarily used by small employers with fewer than 20 employees not subject to federal COBRA
kentucky.who: An individual (and dependents) whose group coverage ends due to termination of membership in the group may continue the group health insurance for up to 18 months after the coverage would otherwise have ended, subject to the statute's eligibility and notice conditions.
kentucky.statute: Ky. Rev. Stat. (KRS) section 304.18-110 (Continuation of group coverage after termination of membership in group -- Notice of eligibility); conversion under KRS 304.18-114
kentucky.market: State-based marketplace: kynect (Kentucky operates its own state exchange platform, not HealthCare.gov).

louisiana.size: group health policies generally (not limited to sub-20 employers); applies where the person is NOT eligible for federal COBRA, so it functions as small-employer state continuation
louisiana.who: An employee or member (and covered dependents) continuously insured under the group policy (or a replaced policy) for the 3 consecutive months before termination, whose coverage would otherwise end due to termination of active employment or membership, death, or divorce. Continuation ends 12 months after coverage would otherwise have terminated.
louisiana.statute: La. Rev. Stat. section 22:1046 (Group health insurance continuation)
louisiana.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Louisiana does not run its own state-based exchange).

maine.size: small group carriers covering employers with fewer than 20 employees are required to offer continuation; the section also provides continuation more broadly on specified triggers
maine.who: A member or employee whose group coverage terminated due to temporary layoff, permanent layoff (where eligible for federal premium assistance), or loss of employment because of a work-related injury/disease may continue coverage for one year (12 months) under 24-A M.R.S. 2809-A(11). The section also covers conversion on termination of policy or eligibility.
maine.statute: 24-A M.R.S. section 2809-A (Conversion on termination of policy or eligibility; continuation), esp. subsection 11
maine.market: State-based marketplace: CoverME.gov (Maine operates its own state exchange platform, not HealthCare.gov).

maryland.law: Maryland Continuation Coverage for Terminated Employees
maryland.size: group health contracts generally; the terminated-employee continuation right is not limited to sub-20 employers, and a related section (15-409.1) addresses small employers specifically
maryland.who: An insured employee who is a Maryland resident and covered under the current or predecessor group contract with the same employer for at least 3 months before a change in status (termination of employment other than for cause) may continue coverage for up to 18 months. A separate section, 15-407, provides continuation for surviving spouses and dependent children (up to 18 months after the.
maryland.statute: Md. Code, Insurance section 15-409 (Continuation coverage for terminated employees); section 15-409.1 (small employers); section 15-407 (surviving spouses/dependents)
maryland.market: State-based marketplace: Maryland Health Connection (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

massachusetts.law: Massachusetts Small Group Continuation of Coverage (Mini-COBRA)
massachusetts.size: small group health benefit plans issued to employers with 2 to 19 eligible employees (below the federal COBRA 20-employee threshold)
massachusetts.who: Employees and eligible dependents (qualified beneficiaries) covered under a small group health plan. Duration by qualifying event: 18 months for termination or reduction in hours (extendable to 29 months on SSA disability determination), and 36 months for death of the covered employee, divorce or legal separation, Medicare entitlement, or a dependent losing eligible status.
massachusetts.market: State-based marketplace: Massachusetts Health Connector (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

michigan.law: none (no small-employer state continuation / mini-COBRA statute; a conversion right exists instead)
michigan.size: not applicable; Michigan does not require small employers (fewer than 20 employees) to offer COBRA-style continuation
michigan.who: No mini-COBRA continuation right. Employees of employers with 20 or more employees rely on federal COBRA. Michigan law instead provides a conversion privilege (right to convert group coverage to an individual policy on a self-pay basis) rather than time-limited group continuation.
michigan.statute: no small-employer continuation statute; MCL 500.3708 is a special-enrollment provision, not continuation; conversion rights exist under the Nonprofit Health Care Corporation Reform Act / Insurance Code
michigan.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Michigan does not run its own state-based exchange).

minnesota.size: every group insurance policy, group subscriber contract, and health care plan (not limited to sub-20 employers); serves as state continuation reaching small employers not subject to federal COBRA
minnesota.who: Every covered employee who is voluntarily or involuntarily terminated or laid off (reduction in hours below eligibility counts as layoff), other than for gross misconduct, may elect to continue coverage for the employee and dependents while the plan remains in force for active employees. Continuation runs until the employee is covered under another group plan or 18 months, whichever is shorter.
minnesota.statute: Minn. Stat. section 62A.17 (Termination of or layoff from employment; continuation and conversion rights)
minnesota.market: State-based marketplace: MNsure (uses its own platform, not HealthCare.gov).

mississippi.size: group policies providing hospital/surgical/major medical coverage generally (not limited to sub-20 employers); functions as state continuation for those outside federal COBRA
mississippi.who: An employee or member (or eligible dependent) continuously insured under the group policy (or a replaced policy) for the 3 consecutive months before termination whose coverage would otherwise end due to termination of employment or membership may continue for up to 12 months. A dependent spouse may continue spouse/child coverage up to 12 months after the employee's death; a dependent child may.
mississippi.statute: Miss. Code section 83-9-51 (Group policy defined; election to continue coverage after employment termination; continuation by dependent spouse or child)
mississippi.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Mississippi does not run its own state-based exchange).

missouri.law: Missouri State Continuation (Federal COBRA provisions applied to group health policies)
missouri.size: group health insurance policies for persons NOT subject to federal COBRA/ERISA continuation (i.e., small employers below the federal COBRA threshold)
missouri.who: Employees or members whose group coverage would otherwise terminate due to termination of employment or membership may continue coverage (including eligible dependents) in the same manner as federal COBRA. Duration mirrors federal COBRA: 18 months for the employee (and dependents on termination or reduction in hours), and 36 months for dependents on death, divorce, or similar events.
missouri.statute: Mo. Rev. Stat. section 376.428 (Federal COBRA provisions to apply to group health insurance policies)
missouri.market: Uses HealthCare.gov (federal marketplace; Missouri does not run its own state-based exchange).

montana.law: Montana group conversion / limited continuation (no fixed-duration mini-COBRA)
montana.size: Group disability (health) insurance policies generally; Montana does not impose a general small-employer state continuation mandate with a fixed maximum duration for terminated employees.
montana.who: On termination of employment or group membership, an insured covered for at least three months is entitled to a CONVERSION policy (individual or group) without evidence of insurability if applied for within 31 days (MCA 33-22-508). Separate limited continuation exists for dependents (MCA 33-22-503), persons with disabilities (MCA 33-22-506), and after a reduction of work schedule (MCA 33-22-507).
montana.statute: Mont. Code Ann. sections 33-22-503, 33-22-506, 33-22-507, 33-22-508 (Part 5, Group Disability Insurance)
montana.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Montana does not run a state-based exchange).

nebraska.size: Group policies covering groups NOT subject to IRC section 4980B (federal COBRA) based on employee count, i.e. small employers under 20 employees, with fully-insured hospital/surgical/major-medical coverage.
nebraska.who: An employee whose coverage would otherwise terminate because of INVOLUNTARY termination of employment for reasons other than misconduct. Coverage continues on a monthly renewal basis until the earliest of: 6 months after coverage would end; eligibility for other group coverage or Medicare; nonpayment; conversion; or termination of the group policy. Interruption due to a labor dispute is not.
nebraska.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Nebraska does not run a state-based exchange).

nevada.size: Group health policies issued to employers with fewer than 20 employees (below the federal COBRA threshold). Federal COBRA governs employers with 20 or more employees.
nevada.who: An employee whose employment is terminated for any reason other than gross misconduct, or whose hours are reduced so they lose eligibility, may elect to continue identical coverage (excluding eye and dental) for up to 18 months. The employee, spouse, or dependent child must have been covered under the employer's group policy for at least 12 consecutive months before coverage ended to be eligible.
nevada.statute: Nev. Rev. Stat. 689B.245 (required continuation provision); Nev. Rev. Stat. 689B.249 (termination of continued coverage before end of period)
nevada.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: Nevada Health Link (https://www.nevadahealthlink.com/).

new-hampshire.size: All fully-insured group health and dental plans in New Hampshire, regardless of employer size (works like COBRA but employees of employers of any size are eligible). The NH Insurance Department has jurisdiction only over fully-insured plans; self-funded plans.
new-hampshire.who: Any insured individual on a fully-insured group plan who loses eligibility due to a qualifying event (termination other than gross misconduct, quit, layoff, reduction in hours, divorce/legal separation, change in dependent status, disability, death of the covered employee, or employer bankruptcy). Length depends on the event: 18 months for job loss; 29 months for Social Security-defined.
new-hampshire.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (New Hampshire does not run a state-based exchange).

new-jersey.law: New Jersey Continuation Coverage (Small Employer Health Benefits continuation)
new-jersey.size: Small employers (2 to 50 eligible employees) with fully-insured group health benefits plans under the NJ Small Employer Health Benefits Program. Provides continuation for those not covered by federal COBRA (federal COBRA generally applies at 20+ employees).
new-jersey.who: Any employee terminated other than for cause, or whose hours are reduced to fewer than 25; plus qualified beneficiary spouses and dependent children. General continuation runs up to 18 months; a spouse or dependent child qualified beneficiary may continue up to 36 months following death of the employee, divorce, or a child ceasing to be a dependent (29 months for a disabled individual).
new-jersey.statute: N.J.S.A. 17B:27A-27 (Small Employer Health Benefits Program continuation)
new-jersey.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: Get Covered New Jersey (https://www.getcovered.nj.gov/).

new-mexico.law: New Mexico Continuation of Coverage and Conversion
new-mexico.size: Group accident and health insurance policies generally (fully-insured). Serves as state continuation for groups not subject to federal COBRA (small employers) as well as a broadly applicable continuation/conversion right.
new-mexico.who: Each member or employee of the insured group whose membership or employment terminates. The policy must offer the right to continue coverage for six months, and thereafter a conversion policy, upon termination of membership or employment.
new-mexico.statute: N.M. Stat. Ann. section 59A-18-16 (Continuation of coverage and conversion rights; accident and health insurance policies; notice)
new-mexico.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: beWellnm (https://www.bewellnm.com/).

new-york.size: Fully-insured group health policies subject to New York Insurance Law, including employers with fewer than 20 employees (below the federal COBRA threshold). New York also extends total continuation to 36 months for anyone eligible for federal COBRA or state.
new-york.who: Employees or members of a group who lose eligibility for coverage, and their covered dependents. Coverage may end early for non-payment, if the employer ceases to maintain any group health plan, if the person becomes covered under another group plan, or upon Medicare entitlement.
new-york.statute: N.Y. Ins. Law sections 3221(m), 4304(k), 4305(e); Chapter 236 and Chapter 498 of the Laws of 2009
new-york.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: NY State of Health (https://nystateofhealth.ny.gov/).

north-carolina.law: North Carolina Group Health Insurance Continuation
north-carolina.size: All fully-insured group health policies (hospital/surgical/major medical) delivered or issued in North Carolina, regardless of employer size; self-insured plans are excluded (G.S. 58-53-115).
north-carolina.who: Employees or members (including insured spouse/dependents) whose coverage would otherwise terminate due to termination of employment or membership, who were continuously insured under the group policy for the three consecutive months immediately before termination. Excludes anyone eligible for other group coverage within 31 days, or who lost coverage for nonpayment.
north-carolina.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (North Carolina does not run a state-based exchange).

north-dakota.size: Fully-insured group hospital/surgical/major medical policies delivered or issued in North Dakota; serves as continuation for employees losing coverage, including those not covered by federal COBRA.
north-dakota.who: Employees or members (and covered spouse/dependents) whose coverage would otherwise terminate due to termination of employment or membership, subject to prior continuous coverage requirements. Continuation runs for thirty-nine weeks.
north-dakota.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (North Dakota does not run a state-based exchange).

ohio.law: Ohio State Continuation of Health Insurance
ohio.size: Fully-insured group health policies; primarily serves employees of employers not subject to federal COBRA (fewer than 20 employees), though the statute is framed around individual eligibility rather than a strict employer-size threshold.
ohio.who: An employee who was continuously insured under the group policy during the entire three-month period preceding termination, whose employment was involuntarily terminated (not for gross misconduct), and who is not otherwise eligible for continuation. Continuation runs up to 12 months after coverage would otherwise terminate.
ohio.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Ohio does not run a state-based exchange).

oklahoma.size: Fully-insured group accident and health policies (and hospital/medical service or HMO contracts). Applies where coverage is NOT subject to federal COBRA; serves as state continuation for smaller groups.
oklahoma.who: An employee (and dependents) whose group insurance terminates for a reason other than termination of the group plan or gross misconduct. The employee has a right to continue coverage for four months following termination of employment (subsection D); a basic 63-day continuation (subsection B) runs concurrently with the four-month period.
oklahoma.statute: 36 O.S. section 4509 (Extension and termination of coverage under group accident and health policy)
oklahoma.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Oklahoma does not run a state-based exchange).

oregon.size: Employers NOT required to offer federal COBRA (generally fewer than 20 employees), with fully-insured group health insurance policies.
oregon.who: A covered person (certificate holder on the day before the qualifying event and during the three-month period ending on that date) and qualified beneficiaries (spouse, dependent child, or a child born/adopted during continuation). Qualifying events: voluntary or involuntary termination of employment, reduction in hours, Medicare eligibility, loss of dependent-child status, termination of group.
oregon.market: Uses a state-based marketplace on the federal platform: the Oregon Health Insurance Marketplace uses HealthCare.gov for enrollment.

pennsylvania.size: Small employers with at least 2 but fewer than 20 employees (below the federal COBRA threshold), whose group health insurance is fully insured.
pennsylvania.who: Covered employees (and their covered dependents) who were continuously insured under the group plan for at least three consecutive months before a qualifying event and who lose coverage due to a qualifying event such as termination of employment (other than for gross misconduct) or reduction in hours. The individual must not be eligible for other group coverage or Medicare.
pennsylvania.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: Pennie (https://pennie.com/).

rhode-island.size: Group hospital, surgical, dental, vision, or medical insurance plans. Members of groups of 50 or fewer pay the carrier directly; those leaving groups of more than 50 pay the employer (or carrier if the workplace ceases to exist). Fully-insured/service-plan.
rhode-island.who: An insured member (plus surviving spouse and covered dependents) whose employment is terminated because of involuntary layoff or death, or as a result of the workplace ceasing to exist, or a permanent reduction in size of the workforce. Continuation is capped at the shorter of 18 months, the length of prior continuous employment, or until the member becomes eligible under another group plan.
rhode-island.statute: R.I. Gen. Laws section 27-19.1-1 (Chapter 27-19.1, Extended Medical Benefits)
rhode-island.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: HealthSource RI (https://healthsourceri.com/).

south-carolina.size: Fully-insured group health plans of employers with fewer than 20 employees (federal COBRA covers 20+). Self-funded plans are excluded regardless of size.
south-carolina.who: Any employee or member (and dependents, spouses/children losing coverage by death, divorce, or loss of dependent status) who has been continuously insured under the same employer's group policy for at least six consecutive months and whose coverage ends for a reason other than nonpayment of premium.
south-carolina.statute: S.C. Code Ann. section 38-71-770 (Mandatory continuation privileges)
south-carolina.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (South Carolina does not run a state-based exchange).

south-dakota.size: Applies broadly to every self-insured health benefit program and every group health insurance policy delivered or issued in South Dakota (commercial insurers, nonprofit service plans, HMOs). No employer-size threshold in the statute.
south-dakota.who: Employees (and eligible dependents) who leave employment or whose coverage is terminated by the insurer (other than termination/replacement of the policy itself). Coverage continues 18 months at the employee's expense; 29 months for a qualified beneficiary determined disabled under Title II or XVI of the Social Security Act during the first 60 days of continuation.
south-dakota.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (South Dakota does not run a state-based exchange).

tennessee.law: Tennessee Continuation of Terminated Group Coverage
tennessee.size: All fully-insured group hospital/surgical/major medical policies delivered or issued in Tennessee, regardless of employer size (applies to commercial insurers, nonprofit insurers, prepaid plans/HMOs, and state government employee plans).
tennessee.who: An employee or member continuously insured under the group policy for at least three months before termination (for any reason other than discontinuance of the group policy or an insured class). General continuation is the fractional policy month plus three additional months; for termination due to divorce or death of the insured spouse, the fractional month plus up to fifteen additional months.
tennessee.statute: Tenn. Code Ann. section 56-7-2312 (Continuation of terminated group coverage; conversion)
tennessee.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Tennessee does not run a state-based exchange).

texas.size: Applies to fully-insured group health plans generally (including small employers with 2 to 19 employees who are not subject to federal COBRA). Small employer health benefit plans must provide continuation under Insurance Code Chapters 1251 and 1271.
texas.who: An employee, member, or dependent whose group coverage terminates for any reason other than involuntary termination for cause (health-related causes do not count as cause), and who was continuously insured under the group policy for at least three consecutive months immediately before termination.
texas.statute: Tex. Ins. Code sections 1251.251-1251.260 (esp. 1251.252 eligibility, 1251.255 duration/termination); HMO parallel at Tex. Ins. Code Chapter 1271
texas.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Texas does not run a state-based exchange).

utah.size: Small employers with 2 to 19 employees (below the federal COBRA threshold) whose group health coverage is fully insured.
utah.who: An employee (and dependents) who loses eligibility under the employer's group policy due to a qualifying event. Coverage may continue up to 12 months at the individual's full cost. Coverage can end early if the insured moves out of state/service area, fails to pay, commits fraud/misrepresentation, becomes eligible for other group coverage, or the employer's coverage terminates.
utah.statute: Utah Code Ann. section 31A-22-722 (Utah mini-COBRA benefits for employer group coverage)
utah.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Utah runs a small-business SHOP-style exchange, Avenue H, but individual coverage uses HealthCare.gov).

vermont.size: Vermont private employers of any size that sponsor a group health plan but are not eligible for federal COBRA (in practice, employers with fewer than 20 employees). Fully-insured group health and dental plans.
vermont.who: Any person whose coverage under a group health insurance plan is terminating because of a qualifying event: loss of employment or reduction in hours; divorce, civil union dissolution, or legal separation; a child ceasing to qualify as a dependent; or death of the covered employee. Not eligible if covered under another group plan or Medicare, or if the job ended due to misconduct.
vermont.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: Vermont Health Connect (https://portal.healthconnect.vermont.gov/).

virginia.law: Virginia Continuation Coverage (Conversion or continuation on termination of eligibility)
virginia.size: Fully-insured group accident and sickness policies where the group policyholder is NOT required to provide federal COBRA continuation (generally employers with 2 to 19 employees).
virginia.who: An insured whose eligibility under the group policy terminates. Present coverage continues for 12 months immediately following termination of eligibility, without evidence of insurability. Conversion to an individual policy is also available.
virginia.statute: Va. Code section 38.2-3541 (Conversion or continuation on termination of eligibility)
virginia.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: Virginia's Insurance Marketplace (Marketplace Virginia / https://www.marketplace.virginia.gov/).

washington.size: Applies to insurers issuing group hospital/medical coverage; the continuation provision is offered to the policyholder (employer) as an option regardless of size. Federal COBRA covers employers with 20+ employees; Washington's continuation option fills the.
washington.who: A person who becomes ineligible for coverage under the group policy (employee, member, spouse, or dependent), where the policyholder elected to include the continuation provision. On termination of continuation, the covered person may convert to an individual policy under RCW 48.21.260.
washington.statute: RCW 48.21.250 (continuation option); RCW 48.21.260 (conversion). Parallel provisions apply to health care service contractors (RCW 48.44) and HMOs (RCW 48.46).
washington.market: Runs its own state-based marketplace: Washington Healthplanfinder (https://www.wahealthplanfinder.org/).

west-virginia.law: West Virginia Continuation (Group Accident and Sickness required policy provision)
west-virginia.size: Fully-insured group accident and sickness policies. The continuation right in section 33-16-3(e) applies to members of an employee group plan; the mini-COBRA notice provisions (33-16-3u) reference small employers with fewer than 20 employees.
west-virginia.who: Members in groups/classes eligible for insurance through an employee's group plan who are INVOLUNTARILY LAID OFF from work may continue to pay premiums at the same group rate and receive the same coverage for a period not to exceed eighteen months.
west-virginia.statute: W. Va. Code section 33-16-3(e) (Required policy provisions); see also section 33-16-3u
west-virginia.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (West Virginia does not run a state-based exchange).

wisconsin.size: Group health insurance policies issued to employers of any size. For employers with 20 or more employees, federal COBRA governs; Wisconsin continuation (Wis. Stat. 632.897) is the state-level requirement that reaches smaller employers below the federal COBRA.
wisconsin.who: A person continuously covered under the group policy for at least 3 months who loses eligibility, including: an employee or member who would otherwise lose coverage; a former spouse whose coverage would end due to divorce or annulment; and the spouse or dependents of a member who dies while covered.
wisconsin.statute: Wis. Stat. section 632.897 (Hospital and medical coverage for persons insured under individual and group policies)
wisconsin.market: Uses the federal marketplace at HealthCare.gov; Wisconsin does not operate its own state-based ACA exchange.

wyoming.size: Non-COBRA group policies (groups not subject to federal COBRA, i.e. generally small employers under 20 employees) with fully-insured hospital/surgical/major medical coverage.
wyoming.who: Employees, members, or covered eligible dependents whose group coverage would otherwise terminate due to termination of employment or membership or loss of eligibility, and who were continuously insured under the group policy for the entire three-month period ending with termination. Not available to those covered by Medicare (excluding spouse/dependents) or another group arrangement.
wyoming.statute: Wyo. Stat. Ann. section 26-19-113 (Continuation of group coverage after termination of employment or membership)
wyoming.market: Uses the federal marketplace, HealthCare.gov (Wyoming does not run a state-based exchange).
